Name: OptimizedLayerSkippingForLLMInference
Title: Optimized Layer Skipping for Efficient Large Language Model Inference on GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic layer skipping, guided by real-time activation energy and spectral analysis, can significantly reduce inference latency without compromising accuracy in large language models (LLMs).
Related Work: Existing works like FlashInfer, SpInfer, and Coruscant focus on optimizing GPU kernels and memory management for efficient LLM inference. However, they do not address the dynamic and context-dependent nature of inference, which can benefit from adaptive layer skipping. Recent works like Spectral Latent Steering and Geometric Signal Separation have explored subspace projections and activation filtering, but they do not provide a hardware-optimized, latency-aware solution for layer skipping.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are computationally intensive, leading to high inference latency. While existing methods optimize GPU kernels and memory management, they often do not account for the dynamic and context-dependent nature of inference. We propose Optimized Layer Skipping for Efficient LLM Inference (OLS-LLM), a hardware-optimized framework that dynamically skips layers based on real-time activation energy and spectral analysis. OLS-LLM uses a lightweight, GPU-friendly algorithm to compute the energy of hidden states and the spectral condition number, identifying layers that can be safely skipped without significant accuracy loss. By dynamically adjusting the number of layers during inference, OLS-LLM reduces wall-clock latency while maintaining high accuracy. We evaluate OLS-LLM on a variety of LLMs and benchmarks, demonstrating significant latency reductions (up to 30%) with minimal accuracy degradation. Our approach is fully reproducible, with detailed profiler traces and benchmarks, and can be integrated into existing LLM serving frameworks.
Experiments: Measure the energy of hidden states and the spectral condition number for each layer in a representative LLM using a lightweight, GPU-friendly algorithm.
Design a dynamic layer-skipping policy that decides which layers to skip based on the computed energy and spectral condition number, ensuring that the skipped layers have minimal impact on accuracy.
Implement the layer-skipping policy in a custom CUDA kernel, ensuring that the overhead of the energy and spectral condition number computation is minimized.
Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets to compare the performance of OLS-LLM with baseline models that do not use layer skipping.
Evaluate the performance of OLS-LLM on a variety of LLMs and benchmarks, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.

Analyze the trade-offs between latency reduction and accuracy degradation, and provide a detailed profiler trace to attribute gains to specific hardware causes (e.g., reduced HBM round-trips, better register allocation).
Risk Factors and Limitations: The dynamic layer-skipping policy may not generalize well to all LLMs and tasks, requiring careful tuning and validation.
The overhead of computing the energy and spectral condition number in real-time may add latency, which needs to be carefully managed.
The framework's effectiveness may be limited by the quality of the spectral analysis and energy computation algorithms, which could introduce inaccuracies.
The reproducibility of results may be impacted by variations in GPU driver versions and hardware configurations, requiring strict control over experimental conditions.
